key_points: In vitro study comparing conventional irrigation (CI), passive ultrasonic irrigation (PUI), and Er:YAG laser activated irrigation (LAI) in root canals accessed via conventional invasive access (CIA) or computer-guided minimally invasive access (MIA) in 66 extracted maxillary first molars.
E. faecalis infection model was established after instrumentation to ProTaper Universal F2 size.

Both PUI and LAI groups showed significantly fewer bacterial CFU counts than conventional irrigation alone (P < 0.05); no significant difference was found between PUI and LAI in root canal lumen disinfection.
For dentin debris bacterial counts after enlargement to F3, the Er:YAG LAI group had the least CFU counts, with a statistically significant antagonism (F = 7.224, P = 0.002).
Minimally invasive access with Er:YAG laser or ultrasonic achieved disinfection comparable to conventional access with the same adjunct.
Conventional irrigation through minimally invasive access (group 2A) was significantly better than through conventional access (group 1A) (P < 0.05).
Er:YAG laser activated irrigation showed superior removal of bacteria from dentinal tubules compared to ultrasonic, and was described as easier to operate.

outcomes.measured: Bacterial CFU counts in root canal lumen after disinfection
outcomes.result: PUI and LAI groups had significantly fewer CFU than CI group (P < 0.05); no significant difference between PUI and LAI (P > 0.05)
outcomes.clinical_significance: Both ultrasonic and Er:YAG laser irrigation improve root canal disinfection over conventional irrigation alone

outcomes.measured: Bacterial CFU counts in dentin debris after enlargement to F3
outcomes.result: Er:YAG LAI group had the least CFU counts; significant antagonism F = 7.224, P = 0.002
outcomes.clinical_significance: Er:YAG laser may be superior to ultrasonic for removing bacteria embedded in dentinal tubules

outcomes.measured: Comparison of disinfection between CIA and MIA subgroups
outcomes.result: No significant difference between CIA and MIA for PUI or LAI subgroups; MIA-CI was significantly better than CIA-CI (P < 0.05)
outcomes.clinical_significance: Er:YAG laser and ultrasonic irrigation compensate for access cavity design limitations in minimally invasive approaches

notes: Study used extracted maxillary first molars with E. faecalis infection model. Instrumentation performed with ProTaper Universal up to F2, then enlarged to F3 for dentin debris collection. Computer-guided minimally invasive access was used for MIA group.

population.condition: Extracted maxillary first molars inoculated with Enterococcus faecalis

superiority_claim: El autor afirma que 'Er:YAG laser is better than ultrasonic in removing bacteria from dentinal tubules and is easy to operate, which is more suitable for minimally invasive root canal treatment.'
source_citation: Shan, X., Tian, F., Li, J., Yang, N., Wang, Y., & Sun, H. (2022). Comparison of Er:YAG laser and ultrasonic in root canal disinfection under minimally invasive access cavity. Lasers in Medical Science. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10103-022-03613-0
clinical_indication: Root canal disinfection under minimally invasive access cavity

drugs_mentioned.name: sodium hypochlorite
drugs_mentioned.dental_relevance: Used as irrigant in all groups during root canal disinfection

uncertainties: Specific Er:YAG laser parameters (wavelength confirmation, power, energy per pulse, frequency, pulse duration, tip size) are not reported in the abstract; full text may contain these but was not available.
Brand/model of Er:YAG laser device not specified in abstract.
Exact irrigation protocol (volume, concentration of NaOCl, application time) not detailed in abstract.
Conflicts of interest and funding sources not mentioned in available metadata.
In vitro model with E. faecalis monoculture may not reflect clinical polymicrobial infection complexity.
